Reserve the first prestige until AFTER the story's first arc resolves so it reads as a chapter break, not a grind reset.

DAILY/SESSION RITUAL via the return-summary + a couple of time-gated systems (raids/events in ADR, replicanti-style slow accruers in AD) so there's always a reason to open the tab once a day — but AVOID hard daily-login streak FOMO mechanics; they fit free-to-play mobile, not a self-contained story RPG and can feel manipulative.

PERMANENT META-PROGRESSION / automation unlocks (Trimps, NGU, AD perks): unlocking automation and quality-of-life as rewards keeps the long tail engaging and signals 'you've grown.' Strongly recommended — frame each as a story-justified capability.

AVOID for a story RPG: competitive leaderboards, PvP, gacha/lootbox randomness on core progression, energy/stamina timers that block play, and aggressive daily-streak guilt loops. These suit live-service mobile monetization and undercut a crafted, single-player narrative.

exemplars.name: A Dark Room
exemplars.what_to_steal: THE blueprint for you. (1) Resource-reveals-panel: every new resource silently unlocks a new building/store panel, so the UI literally grows out of play. (2) One-button cold open ('stoke the fire') with a scrolling event log beside it so the screen is never dead. (3) Mechanic reveals delivered AS story beats through that log (the stranger arriving = the build menu appearing). (4) Whole-game genre shift (idle survival -> village sim -> RPG world map + combat -> sci-fi reveal) proving a UI can metamorphose with the plot. (5) The Amir Rajan lesson: seed timed narrative messages in the sparse early minutes or players bounce off the empty screen.

exemplars.name: Universal Paperclips
exemplars.what_to_steal: Wholesale UI metamorphosis at phase boundaries — three completely different interfaces (business sim -> ops/trust strategy -> abstract space conquest) map perfectly onto story Acts. The 'Projects' grid that fills in as prerequisites are met (upgrades that unlock NEW mechanics/panels, not just multipliers). The 'Trust' capacity-gating where a meta-resource limits how many systems you can have active — make UI growth a player choice. Tight fiction that gives the numbers meaning end to end.

exemplars.name: Cookie Clicker
exemplars.what_to_steal: Cost-curve math (1.15 multiplier, ~5x jumps between tiers, base costs 15/100/1100...). The grayed-out next-building pattern: always show exactly one locked-but-priced item as the visible savings goal, hide the one after. Achievements-as-guide with small permanent bonuses. Building synergies (cross-multipliers) for late-game depth. Two-buttons-at-start restraint.

exemplars.name: Kittens Game
exemplars.what_to_steal: Tab-per-system reveal with precise resource/research triggers (minerals -> Workshop tab; Calendar research -> Time tab). The growing tab BAR as part of the incremental UI. Deep simulation hidden behind a deceptively simple text UI — but ALSO a cautionary tale on complexity creep (see pitfalls).

exemplars.name: Trimps
exemplars.what_to_steal: Progressive AUTOMATION as the reward structure: you manually do something, then earn the toggle to automate it, freeing attention for the next manual layer — this is how to pace the active->idle transition. Permanent automation unlocks as you climb zones. Natural session break points (early progress in minutes, later in hours).

exemplars.name: NGU Idle
exemplars.what_to_steal: 'Modular ever-expanding UI where new tabs unlock per boss defeated' — milestone-gated tab proliferation. Resource-allocation as the core decision (Energy/Magic split across features), and the namesake NGU infinite-but-diminishing upgrade sink for the very long tail. Bosses (story beats) as the unlock currency maps cleanly to a story RPG's progression gates.

exemplars.name: Melvor Idle
exemplars.what_to_steal: Skill/feature unlocks gated behind level AND an increasing GP cost (Adventure Mode), forcing a deliberate unlock ORDER and giving the player meaningful sequencing choices. Skills that feed each other (mine ore -> smith gear -> combat) as an interdependence web. Explicitly non-competitive, set-your-own-goals framing suited to single-player.

exemplars.name: Antimatter Dimensions
exemplars.what_to_steal: Sequential reveal of a row of producers (dimension 1 -> 2 -> ... -> 8), then second-order systems (Dimension Boosts, Galaxies, Tickspeed) appearing only once you've mastered the first, then the Infinity prestige. Clean nested prestige layers (Infinity/Eternity/Reality) with published, deliberately escalating pacing (<1 day / <1 week / ~1 month). The moment scientific notation 'turns on' as its own milestone.

pitfalls: Empty-screen bounce: the cold-open one-button minimalism that makes ADR great also nearly tanked its iOS launch (feared 1-star reviews from players seeing only 'stoke fire'). If the early UI is sparse, you MUST fill the void with narrative/event messages and a fast first reveal, or players quit in the first 60 seconds.

Progression walls: an economy too tight -> player hits a multi-hour wait with nothing new to do and churns; too generous -> player maxes out and there's nothing left to chase. Never let the next visible goal balloon to many multiples of the previous wait without injecting a NEW mechanic. Rule of thumb: introduce a new system rather than asking for raw patience.
Automation timing errors: automate a chore too early and the player disengages (nothing to do); too late and they churn from tedium. The active->idle handoff must be tuned per system.

Prestige-reset feel-bad: context-free 'reset everything for a multiplier' clashes badly with narrative ('why redo the story?') and can make players feel they're grinding in circles. In a story RPG, resets must be diegetic (reincarnation/time-loop/new era) and carry meaning, or skipped early.

Complexity creep / spreadsheet syndrome (Kittens Game's own community flags this): late-game optimal play degenerating into monstrous calculations alienates casual/story-seeking players. Keep decisions legible; resist stacking interacting multipliers faster than you teach them.
Mistaking bigger numbers for content: players re-hook on NEW mechanics, not larger values. A long stretch of 'same loop, bigger number' with no new panel/system is where idle games lose people.

Narrative-mechanic desync: if the story advances on a separate track from the systems, the fiction feels bolted-on. ADR/Paperclips work because each mechanic reveal IS a story beat. Tie them or the narrative becomes ignorable flavor text.
Offline progress that trivializes the story: if plot or key unlocks accrue offline, players return to find decisions already made and tension drained. Let grind idle; gate STORY on active play.

Notation/legibility shock: dumping scientific notation or dozens of resources on the player at once is overwhelming. Reveal magnitude and resource count gradually; the formatting change should be a celebrated milestone, not a sudden wall of e-notation.

Tutorial over-explaining: the genre's joy is discovery ('revelatory moments are more fulfilling when not spelled out' — ADR's Townsend). Heavy hand-holding tutorials kill the very surprise the UI-reveal design exists to create. Teach through the reveal itself.
lessons_for_us: Make the UI-reveal engine a first-class system in the pure core: represent every panel/tab/resource-row/button as DATA with an `unlock` predicate over game state (flags, resources, level, story node). The renderer just shows what's currently unlocked. This satisfies your pure-core boundary AND makes 'the UI is incremental' a tunable content table rather than hardcoded view logic.

Adopt A Dark Room's resource-reveals-panel as the default unlock idiom: the first time a resource/stat appears, it lights up its associated panel — and announce that reveal as a story event in a persistent message/log component that exists from second one (so the screen is never empty).

Open with literally one interactive element plus the event log. Get the first action under 5 seconds, the first automated producer under ~30 seconds, and aim for 5-8 distinct 'new thing appeared' reveals in the first 20-30 minutes. This window decides retention.

Fuse every mechanic reveal to a story beat. A panel/tab/automation should never appear silently; it arrives because something happened in the narrative. Treat the unlock table and the story-flag graph as the same dependency graph.

Use the genre's proven math as defaults: producer cost `base * 1.15^owned`, ~5x base-cost jumps between tiers, linear/polynomial production vs exponential cost. Generate the resulting balance/time-to-milestone tables into docs/ (per your 'generate, don't duplicate' rule) so pacing is auditable and you can spot walls before players do.

Structure the whole game as Acts that can RE-LAYOUT the UI (Paperclips-style), not just append panels. Each Act = a chapter with its own dominant systems and possibly a diegetic reset carrying meta-progress forward. This turns prestige into plot.

Pace automation unlocks as Trimps does: let the player do each new thing manually first, then GIVE them automation as a story-justified reward, freeing attention for the next layer. This is your active->idle dial.

Pick retention mechanics that fit a single-player story: capped offline grind + 'while you were away' summary, achievements-as-guide with small permanent bonuses, and diegetic prestige. Explicitly avoid leaderboards, gacha, energy timers, and daily-streak FOMO.
Gate STORY on active play, GRIND on idle: never advance plot or hand out narrative unlocks offline, so returning players still get to make the meaningful choices themselves.

Lean on discovery over tutorials: teach through the reveal, keep one known-unknown dangling at all times (a grayed locked item/tab with a teaser), and let players feel clever — that surprise is the product. Build a DEV-only window play-API (per your capture-game-states skill) to headlessly fast-forward and verify that each unlock fires at the intended state, so you can regression-test the reveal pacing.

setting.social_structure:
The Edo period (1603-1868) is popularly framed by the shi-no-ko-sho idea: samurai (warriors/officials), no (farmers), ko (artisans), sho (merchants). IMPORTANT for authenticity: modern scholarship (since ~1995, now reflected in Japanese textbooks) treats this NOT as a rigid four-rung ladder but as a Confucian CONCEPTUAL ordering. The real hard line ran between the ruling warrior class (~5-7% of the population, the only ones allowed a public surname plus the daisho pair of swords) and everyone else, the commoners. Farmer/artisan/merchant were a CLASSIFICATION of commoners, not a strict pecking order, so avoid rendering it as a clean caste ladder.

The load-bearing relationship for the game is samurai-ruler vs peasant-producer. Peasants were ~80-85% of the population and paid tax in RICE (measured in koku); rice yield was the literal basis of samurai power, since a domain's wealth and a samurai's stipend were both denominated in koku. This makes farming the natural incremental base resource that everything else taxes or feeds off.

The nuance that fits your premise perfectly is the GOSHI (country samurai) and the rural landed elite, who blur the warrior/commoner line. A countryside estate is most plausibly headed by a goshi or a wealthy landholder holding samurai-adjacent privilege (surname, sword, partial tax exemption) earned through service or inherited from a warrior ancestor who settled the land generations ago. Below him the village self-administered through three commoner offices: the nanushi/shoya (village headman, usually the richest farmer, who collected tax and relayed the lord's orders), the kumigashira (ward/group heads), and the hyakushodai (peasant representative who watched the headman on the villagers' behalf). Peasant society itself was stratified: honbyakusho (landholding 'main' peasants with village voting rights) sat above mizunomi-byakusho ('water-drinking', i.e. landless tenants and day-laborers) and indentured servant households (genin/fudai). A 17-year-old farmhand belongs near the bottom of this ladder: a hired hand or servant with no land, no public surname, and no sword. That low status is good design fuel, the whole arc can be about quietly earning standing, trust, and eventually privileges normally reserved for those above you.
setting.estate_setting:
A countryside warrior residence is a BUKE YASHIKI, but a RURAL goshi estate is humbler and more farm-like than the famous castle-town samurai blocks. Picture a walled or hedged compound: an earthen or plastered mud wall (or a thick living hedge / bamboo grove) enclosing the grounds, entered through a roofed gate (mon) whose scale signals rank. Inside is a swept earth/gravel forecourt, the main house (omoya) with a thatched (kayabuki) or tiled roof, raised tatami rooms for the family and guests, a large earthen-floored work area (doma) with the kamado cooking hearth and a sunken irori hearth in the living room. Surrounding it: a kura (thick-walled, fire-resistant storehouse for rice, tools, and valuables), stables/byre for an ox or horse, a well, a vegetable garden and persimmon/plum trees, a small private garden for the family, and a household shrine (kamidana inside, plus a small outdoor inari or ujigami shrine). A homestead woodland (yashikirin) of useful trees often rings the house. Just beyond lie the estate's own rice paddies and dry fields worked by tenants and hired hands.

Plausible NPC roster: the master / goshi family head (your distant authority and the mystery's center); his wife / okamisan (runs the household economy); the heir son (rival or ally); a daughter (possible confidante); the elderly retired patriarch (inkyo, keeper of family history and secrets); the head retainer / steward (banto who manages the estate); a swordmaster or guard who drills the household; the village headman (nanushi) who liaises with the estate; senior farmhands and tenant-farmer families; the kitchen maids and a head housekeeper; the stable hand / oxherd; a gardener; a wet-nurse or old nanny full of folk tales; a resident or visiting priest (kannushi at the local shrine) and a Buddhist monk from the family temple (bodaiji); itinerant figures who pass through (peddler/gyosho, medicine seller, a komuso flute-monk, a ronin); and craft specialists tied to the land (the village smith, a charcoal-burner, a sake/miso brewer). One of these becomes your GUIDE, most naturally a kind senior farmhand, the old nanny, or the headman's child who takes pity on the confused newcomer.
daily_life_and_trades: FARMING (core gathering loop): wet-rice paddy cultivation is the centerpiece, a seasonal cycle of seedling beds, flooding/transplanting (taue), weeding, harvest, threshing, and hulling. Dry fields add barley, millet, soybeans, daikon, and vegetables. Rice doubles as the world's base currency (koku). Green/leaf-litter fertilizer gathered from the satoyama and night-soil/ash recycling fit a 'nothing wasted' upgrade tree.

FORAGING / sansai (mountain vegetables): seasonal gathering of warabi/zenmai (bracken ferns), bamboo shoots, mushrooms (matsutake as a rare high-value drop), wild greens (fuki, seri), chestnuts, walnuts, persimmons, and medicinal herbs. Maps cleanly to a tiered, season-gated gathering node system.

FISHING & river/coast harvest: river and pond fishing (ayu/sweetfish, carp, eel, loach), weirs and traps, plus freshwater shellfish and, near water, duck/waterfowl. Eel and ayu are prized; a fishing minigame or node tier fits naturally.

WOODCUTTING & forestry: coppicing the village woodland for firewood and construction timber, with judicious thinning, an Edo-era sustainable-forestry practice. Bamboo harvesting is a distinct, fast-renewing resource for baskets, tools, and building.

CHARCOAL BURNING (sumiyaki): converting cordwood in earth kilns into charcoal, an essential fuel and a classic remote-forest trade, great as a 'processing' craft that turns gathered wood into a higher-value good (and powers the smithy).

SMITHING / metalwork: the village blacksmith makes and repairs farm tools, sickles, hoes, knives, and nails; charcoal feeds the forge. A crafting tree from raw iron/charcoal to tools to fine blades supports a satisfying tech ladder (and gates later weapons).

WEAVING, SPINNING & DYEING: women spun hemp/ramie (and silk where sericulture existed) and hand-loomed cloth; INDIGO (ai) dyeing was iconic and practical, believed to repel snakes/insects and hide field stains, and one of the few colors commoners were permitted. Boro patchwork mending fits a thrift/repair mechanic. A textile chain: fiber to thread to woven cloth to dyed garments.
SERICULTURE (silkworm raising): seasonal mulberry-leaf gathering and silkworm tending to produce raw silk, a high-value regional cash crop and a good mid-game economic upgrade.
PAPERMAKING (washi): kozo/mulberry-bark processing into handmade paper, a refined craft useful for trade goods, lanterns, and shoji.
POTTERY & LACQUERWARE: clay-dug ceramics (everyday bowls/jars, plus storage urns for the kura) and urushi lacquerwork, slow high-skill crafts that yield prestige goods.

BREWING & food processing: sake (rice + koji), miso, soy sauce, and pickling (tsukemono); rice-hulling and milling. These convert farm output into stored, tradeable value, a clean 'food/processing' branch of crafting.

HUNTING & trapping: in mountain districts, matagi-style hunting of boar, deer, and game birds (for meat, hide, antler), plus snaring small game, a higher-risk frontier trade that bridges gathering into the dangerous folklore-rich uplands.
CARPENTRY & thatching: woodworking for tools, furniture, buildings, and roof-thatch (kaya grass cut from managed grasslands), a construction craft that can gate base/estate upgrades.
folklore_and_myth: KAPPA: river/pond water-imp with a water-filled dish on its head; drowns the careless and steals cucumbers. Ideal early water-area mob and a source of cautionary lore around paddies and ponds.

KITSUNE (fox spirits): shapeshifting tricksters AND divine messengers of Inari, the rice deity, so they tie directly to the farming theme and the local shrine. Use for illusion puzzles, a benevolent-or-malevolent quest-giver, and a possible thread in the central mystery.
TANUKI: jovial shapeshifting raccoon-dog of the satoyama woodlands; mischief, disguise, and trickery. Great comic-relief mob or a transforming NPC.

TENGU: mountain spirits, bird/long-nosed, masters of swordsmanship and guardians of high passes and the boundary to the otherworld. Classic late-game mountain boss and, critically, the folkloric agent of being 'spirited away', a direct hook to the protagonist's arrival.

YAMAUBA / YAMANBA (mountain hag) and YAMABITO (mountain hermits): dwellers of the deep forest, dangerous old-woman witch and reclusive mountain folk. Good for an eerie deep-forest area boss/encounter.
ONI: horned ogre-demons, the heavy-hitter brutes of the uplands and old battlefields; a staple dungeon/boss enemy and the target of ritual purification (setsubun bean-throwing) lore.

INARI / UJIGAMI and the kami of the land: the local tutelary kami and the rice god venerated at the estate's shrine; not enemies but the spiritual backbone, source of blessings, festivals, omikuji, and quests to restore neglected shrines.

ZASHIKI-WARASHI: a child-like house spirit that brings fortune to the home it haunts and ruin when it leaves, perfect as an estate-bound mystery presence and a soft tie to the household's luck and your arrival.
YUREI (vengeful ghosts): white-robed spirits with unfinished business; great for haunted-site quests, graveyard areas, and emotionally weighted late-game mysteries.
NOPPERA-BO (faceless ghost) and ROKUROKUBI (long-necked woman): unsettling night-road and household yokai for atmospheric scares and 'something is wrong with this NPC' reveals.
NURIKABE: an invisible wall yokai that blocks night travelers, a fun environmental/obstacle yokai gating a path until solved.
JOROGUMO: spider-woman who lures victims to her web, a seductive-trap boss for a deep-forest or abandoned-house lair.
GASHADOKURO: a giant skeleton assembled from the bones of the war-dead and the starved, an apocalyptic late-game boss tied to old battlefields and famine lore.

KAMIKAKUSHI / TENGU-KAKUSHI ('hidden by the kami/tengu'): not a monster but the FOLK EXPLANATION for sudden disappearances, recorded in Yanagita Kunio's Tono Monogatari. Victims sometimes return years later, utterly changed. This is the single best folkloric frame for your isekai-flavored central mystery.
geography_and_areas: THE ESTATE & its grounds (hub): the walled compound, forecourt, kura storehouse, household shrine, garden, and well, the safe home base and quest hub.
THE PADDY & FIELD belt (sato): the estate's wet-rice paddies, dry fields, irrigation channels, and tenant cottages, the first gathering zone (farming) and home to kappa/paddy-spirit lore.
THE VILLAGE (mura): clustered farmhouses around the headman's house, the smith, a small market/crossroads, and the communal well, the social/trade hub with NPCs, rumors, and the festival ground.

THE CHINJU-NO-MORI (sacred shrine grove): the wooded local Shinto shrine at the foot of a hill, torii gate, fox/Inari statues, ema plaques, the spiritual node for blessings, omens, and kitsune encounters.
THE SATOYAMA WOODLAND (coppice forest): managed secondary forest for firewood, charcoal, foraging mushrooms/sansai, and bamboo groves, the mid-tier gathering zone where tanuki and minor yokai dwell.
THE RIVER & PONDS: the irrigation source, fishing weirs, a watermill, a bridge and ferry crossing, fishing zone and kappa territory; follow it upstream toward the mountains.
THE FAMILY TEMPLE (bodaiji) & graveyard: the Buddhist temple the estate is registered to, with its monk, bell, and ancestral graves, ties to death/ghost (yurei) content and family history clues.

THE HIGHWAY / KAIDO & post-stop: a road passing nearby with a teahouse, a stone jizo statue, milestones, and travelers (peddlers, monks, ronin), the conduit for outside news, trade, and wandering NPCs.
THE FOOTHILLS & TERRACED slopes: terraced paddies climbing into rougher land, abandoned fields, hunting trails, the transition into wilder, more dangerous territory.

THE DEEP MOUNTAINS (okuyama) & PASS (toge): misty high forest, waterfalls, a derelict mountain shrine, charcoal-burners' huts, and a treacherous pass, the late-game frontier of tengu, yamauba, oni, and the threshold of the 'otherworld' where kamikakushi happens, a fitting place for the mystery's climax.
ABANDONED / liminal sites: a ruined manor, a plague-emptied hamlet, an old battlefield, or a cave, optional dungeon-like areas hosting yurei, gashadokuro, jorogumo, and the darker lore.
story_hooks: KAMIKAKUSHI return: the protagonist is locally believed to be a child who was 'spirited away' by a tengu/kami years ago and has now mysteriously returned, aged and amnesiac. The estate took them in out of duty/superstition; the mystery is whether they truly are that lost child, and what the otherworld did to them. This grounds the isekai hook in genuine Edo folklore (Yanagita's Tono Monogatari).

The zashiki-warashi bargain: the estate's fortunes have been quietly held up by a house spirit; the protagonist arrived the same night its presence shifted. Restoring or appeasing it (and learning why YOU appeared in its place) becomes the through-line, tying the mystery to the household's prosperity, an incremental-friendly fortune mechanic.

The shrine that was let fall: the local ujigami/Inari shrine has been neglected and a boundary between worlds is thinning, which is why you slipped through. Re-consecrating shrines area by area both explains your arrival and structures map progression toward the mountain where the breach is widest.

The fox's debt: a kitsune (Inari's messenger) pulled or sent you to settle an old wrong the estate's ancestors did to the land/foxes; the guide and the master each know fragments. Trickster-fox quests reveal the truth in pieces and let folklore drive the plot.

The double / the missing heir: you wear the face of a family member who vanished (the kamikakushi victim, a dead heir, or the master's runaway child). Whether you are a returnee, an impostor, a reincarnation, or something a tengu wears is the central question; NPC trust and your earned standing gate the reveals.

Across a famine/disaster threshold: you 'woke' having crossed from a time of famine, fire, or plague (real Edo calamities). The estate is on the eve of, or recovering from, such a disaster, and your foreknowledge or out-of-place memories are both a resource and the clue to how/why you crossed over.

The tengu's apprentice: you were taken to the mountains, trained/altered by a tengu, and released with skills you don't remember learning (explaining how a farmhand can fight/level). Reclaiming those memories pulls you back up the mountain toward a final confrontation at the pass.

people: Note: in public, Edo commoners used a given name plus their village or trade (e.g. 'Tasuke of Mizuho village'); only the warrior/landed family would bear a surname. Men (peasant/servant): Tarokichi, Sahei, Kanbei, Rokusuke, Yasukichi, Tasuke, Heita, Gonzo, Matakichi, Kyubei (birth-order names like Taro/first, Jiro/second, Saburo/third are very common).
Women (peasant/servant): often one or two syllables, sometimes with the polite O- prefix, e.g. Oharu, Okiku, Osen, Onatsu, Oyuki, Otami, Okane, Oshige, Otsuyu.

Estate/goshi family (surname allowed): Akiyama Genzaemon (head), his wife Onobu, heir Akiyama Shinnosuke, daughter Akiyama Sayo, retired patriarch Akiyama Soan (a Buddhist retirement name), steward Yagoro.
Religious/wandering: the shrine priest Kannushi Mitsuomi; the temple monk Ryokai or Tetsugen; a komuso flute-monk; a ronin named Kuranosuke or Hanbei; a peddler called Tokubei.
Plausible estate surnames (land/nature-derived, in the farmer-name tradition): Akiyama, Sugimoto, Kuroda, Nogami, Mizuno, Tachibana, Yamashiro, Sakai, Hara, Fuyuki.
places: Villages (suffix -mura/-sato): Mizuho-mura ('abundant rice'), Inaba-mura, Kasuga-mura, Yamanaka-mura, Kogarashi-mura, Higashiyama-mura, Tobishima-mura.
Natural/landscape names: Tsukikage Pond, Kappa-buchi (kappa pool), Senzu River, Hotaru-no-sawa (firefly marsh), Akinashi Plain, Kareha Woods, Susuki-no-hara (pampas grassland).
Uplands/passes: Ookami-toge (Wolf Pass), Tengu-dake (Tengu Peak), Kumo-no-taira, Yamiyama ('dark mountain'), Shirakumo Falls, Oku-no-mori (the deep forest).

Sacred/built sites: Inari-jinja (the fox shrine), Chinju-no-mori (the tutelary grove), Komorebi-an (a hermitage), Jizo-zaka (the jizo slope on the highway), Kanezawa-ji (the family temple), Furumiya ('the old shrine').
The estate itself could be known simply as 'the Akiyama yashiki' or by its locale, e.g. 'the manor at Mizuho'.

authenticity_notes: Don't present shi-no-ko-sho as a hard caste ladder; it was a Confucian ideal and a classification of commoners. The real divide was warrior vs commoner. Showing the goshi/village-headman gray zone is both more accurate and richer.

Religion was syncretic (shinbutsu shugo): the same household honored Shinto kami (kamidana, the ujigami/Inari shrine) AND Buddhist ancestors (the family bodaiji temple, butsudan altar). Don't split them into rival 'factions'; villagers used both as one continuous practice.

Yokai and the supernatural were genuinely woven into ordinary belief, not just spooky set-dressing. Treat them as part of how rural people explained weather, illness, disappearances, and luck, which makes the folklore feel lived-in rather than gimmicky.

Avoid samurai-fantasy cliches at a farm estate: a rural goshi is closer to landed gentry/yeoman than to a katana-duelist; most 'samurai' by mid-Edo were impoverished bureaucrats, and a country estate runs on agriculture, not constant swordfights. Let combat be the exception (yokai, bandits, the mountains), not the daily texture.
Get the economy right: tax and wealth are reckoned in rice (koku); coins coexist but rice is the deep unit. This is both authentic and great incremental-game scaffolding.

Respect the labor: peasant life was hard, communal, and dignified, not quaint. Foraging, mending (boro), recycling night-soil and ash, and sustainable coppicing reflect real ingenuity, lean into that rather than romanticizing poverty or playing it for misery.

Be careful with sacred and sensitive elements: shrines, kami, ancestor rites, and burial customs are living traditions for many people today. Represent rituals respectfully and avoid turning real deities (e.g. Inari) into trivial 'loot pinatas.'

Naming: keep public commoner identification as given-name-plus-place/trade, and reserve surnames for the warrior/landed family; use a Buddhist 'retirement name' (e.g. -an / -soan) for an elder who has formally retired (inkyo). This small detail signals real social literacy.

Geography: anchor the world in the satoyama mosaic (village + paddies + managed woodland + sacred grove + mountains). It is the historically correct rural template and conveniently maps to tiered game zones.

Source caveat: many details here come from general/secondary web sources; before locking lore, sanity-check specifics (regional variation is large, sericulture, fishing, and crops differed a lot by region and decade across the 265-year period) and consider a sensitivity read from someone versed in Japanese history/folklore.
